measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a one-year maintenance and operations levy for the Washtucna Pool.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named local-service funding; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a one-year property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a one-year maintenance and operations levy for the Lind Swimming Pool.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named local-service funding; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a one-year property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a one-year maintenance and operations levy for the Ritzville Water Park.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named local-service funding; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a one-year property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a one-year maintenance and operations levy for cemetery upkeep.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named local-service funding; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a one-year property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Continues a rural emergency-medical-services property-tax levy for six years beginning with 2027 collections.
measures.cost_line: The maximum rate rises from $0.15 to $0.28 per $1,000 assessed value, equal to $140 annually on $500,000 at the maximum.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies continued rural EMS as the use; no committee argument was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee argument was available; the direct tradeoff is continued EMS funding at an increased property-tax rate.

taxes.basis: YES continues the levy for six years and raises its maximum property-tax rate.

measures.sources_note: Official sample ballot and county election page (S1, S2).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a one-year excess property-tax levy for Clarkston ambulance and EMS maintenance and operations in 2027.
measures.cost_line: The levy raises $1,217,828 at approximately $1.540 per $1,000 assessed value, or about $770 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies ambulance and EMS maintenance and operations as the use; no committee argument was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee argument was available; the direct tradeoff is one year of EMS operating revenue versus the excess property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a one-year excess property-tax levy at the stated approximate rate.

measures.sources_note: Official sample ballot and county election page (S1, S2).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the regular levy from about $1.31 to up to $1.50 per $1,000 in 2027, permits a 106% limit factor for nine years, and resets the later levy base.
measures.cost_line: The pro committee estimates about $5.54 monthly for a $350,000 home.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says restoration supports six firefighters, an engine, water rescue, prevention, equipment, facilities, response times, and reliability.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted; the direct trade-off is the rate and growth authority versus service and capital funding.

taxes.basis: YES raises the rate, allows up to 6% growth for nine years, and resets the base.

spending.basis: YES expands staffing and capital capacity.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet and pro statement (S1); no con submitted.

measures.what_it_does: Reestablishes for ten years a dedicated EMS levy up to $0.50 per $1,000 beginning in 2027.
measures.cost_line: Up to $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: The official explanation says reestablishment maintains EMS personnel, equipment, facilities, ambulance service, and current service amid rising costs; no pro statement was submitted.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted; the trade-off is a ten-year dedicated property tax versus EMS continuity.

taxes.basis: YES reestablishes a ten-year dedicated property tax.

spending.basis: YES continues a restricted EMS program funding personnel, equipment, facilities, ambulance service, and current service levels.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet explanation (S1); no pro or con statement submitted.

measures.what_it_does: Creates a dedicated ten-year property-tax levy for basic and advanced emergency medical services beginning with 2027 collections.
measures.cost_line: Up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value for ten consecutive years; approval requires the statutory supermajority and turnout thresholds.
measures.pro_summary: The district says dedicated revenue would separate EMS funding from the general fire levy and support staffing as emergency-call demand grows.
measures.con_summary: No official opposition statement was available; the direct tradeoff is a new dedicated property-tax charge for ten years.

taxes.basis: YES creates a new dedicated property-tax levy of up to $0.50 per $1,000 for ten years.

spending.basis: YES funds dedicated EMS operations and additional staffing as the service-expansion lever.

measures.sources_note: Official sample ballot (S1) and local reporting on the district's funding rationale (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Renews East County Fire & Rescue's emergency-medical-services property-tax levy for six years beginning in 2027, at no more than $0.35 per $1,000 of assessed value; the current levy expires at the end of 2026.
measures.cost_line: Up to $140 per year on $400,000 assessed value, $227.50 on $650,000, or $350 on $1 million.
measures.pro_summary: The submitted pro committee says renewal maintains dedicated funding for paramedics, EMTs, ambulances, equipment, and training and continues rather than creates the levy.
measures.con_summary: No person volunteered to submit a con statement or rebuttal, so the official pamphlet contains no organized opposition argument.

taxes.basis: YES continues a dedicated property-tax levy for six years, but at an existing rather than newly added rate.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet resolution summary, explanatory statement, and submitted pro statement (S1); no con committee material was filed.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes up to $15.2 million in general-obligation bonds, maturing within 20 years, to construct and equip a replacement fire station, with excess property taxes authorized for repayment.
measures.cost_line: The district estimates $0.45 per $1,000 of assessed value, about $292.50 annually or $24.38 monthly for a $650,000 home.
measures.pro_summary: The submitted pro committee says the 1963 station has water damage, asbestos, obsolete infrastructure, earthquake damage, and inadequate space, and that assessments found replacement more responsible than retrofit.
measures.con_summary: No person volunteered to submit a con statement or rebuttal, so the official pamphlet contains no organized opposition argument.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes an excess property-tax levy over as many as 20 years to repay $15.2 million in bonds.

spending.basis: YES makes a major capital investment in replacing and equipping an aging fire station.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet resolution summary, explanatory statement, and submitted pro statement (S1); no con committee material was filed.

measures.what_it_does: Continues an EMS property-tax levy of up to $0.50 per $1,000 for six years beginning with 2027 collections.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum rate, $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies continued emergency-medical-service funding as the purpose; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No official opposition statement was available; the tradeoff is continued EMS funding versus the six-year property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing local property-tax levy for six years.

measures.sources_note: Official sample ballot and resolution index (S1, S2); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Renews a one-year excess levy raising $1,460,000 to maintain, develop, and expand district health-care facilities and services.
measures.cost_line: The estimated 2027 rate is $0.33 per $1,000: $33 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $165 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material says the levy maintains essential health-care availability and funds maintenance, development, and expansion.
measures.con_summary: No official opposition statement was available; the tradeoff is health-care investment versus a one-year excess property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES renews a one-year excess property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES expressly funds maintenance, development, and expansion of health-care facilities and services.

measures.sources_note: Official sample ballot and resolution index (S1, S2); no opposition statement was available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes regular property-tax levies for six years to fund emergency medical services in Republic.
measures.cost_line: $0.50 per $1,000 assessed value, or $250 annually on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies Republic EMS as the use; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the direct tradeoff is EMS funding versus six years of property tax.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a six-year regular property-tax levy for EMS.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Continues the district's regular EMS levy for six years, collected from 2027 through 2032 outside Republic.
measures.cost_line: $0.50 per $1,000 assessed value, or $250 annually on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies district EMS as the use; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the direct tradeoff is continued EMS funding versus six years of property tax.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing six-year regular property-tax levy for EMS.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a multi-year levy lid lift for expanded health-care services and facilities at Mattawa Community Medical Clinic.
measures.cost_line: Sets a maximum 2027 regular property-tax rate of $0.75 per $1,000, permits annual levy growth up to 6% through 2035, and uses the 2035 levy as the later statutory base.
measures.pro_summary: The ballot title states that revenue is limited to expanded local health-care services and facilities.
measures.con_summary: No official pro or con committee statements were available; the stated tradeoff is the property-tax increase and long-term levy growth.

taxes.basis: YES raises the regular property-tax levy, allows up to 6% annual growth through 2035, and permanently resets the levy base.

spending.basis: YES funds expanded public health-care services and facilities as the primary lever for local access.

measures.sources_note: Official sample-ballot title and fiscal terms (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a four-year excess levy raising $200,000 annually for ambulance maintenance and operations.
measures.cost_line: Approximately $0.45 per $1,000, or $45 annually per $100,000 and $225 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies ambulance maintenance and operations as the use; no pro statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was available; the tradeoff is ambulance funding versus higher property taxes.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a substantial property-tax levy or debt obligation.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Renews a six-year EMS levy at $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value.
measures.cost_line: $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies EMS operations as the use; no pro statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was available; the tradeoff is continued EMS funding versus continued property taxes.

taxes.basis: YES continues or renews an existing property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes $10 million in bonds, repayable over no more than 25 years, to replace the headquarters fire station.
measures.cost_line: Up to $10 million principal plus interest through excess property-tax levies over no more than 25 years; no model-home estimate is stated.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies replacement headquarters construction as the use; no pro statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was available; the tradeoff is a replacement station versus long-term property-tax debt service.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a substantial property-tax levy or debt obligation.

spending.basis: YES makes a substantial public-service or capital investment.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Restores the library district's regular property-tax levy to $0.47 per $1,000 for 2027 and establishes that collection as the base for later statutory levy limits.
measures.cost_line: The official pamphlet estimates an increase of $124.25 annually, or $10.35 monthly, for a home assessed at $748,100.
measures.pro_summary: The official pro statement says the restoration protects literacy programs, hours, digital access, bookmobile and student support, and collections.
measures.con_summary: The official con statement describes a 55% rate increase, questions budget growth since 2018, and argues for reassessing services and priorities before raising taxes.

taxes.basis: YES restores a higher property-tax levy rate and resets the base used for future statutory limits.

spending.basis: YES expands the revenue base for general library operations and maintenance rather than requiring service reprioritization within current revenue.

measures.sources_note: Official Island County pamphlet (S0), district resolution (S1), and district levy information (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the cemetery levy to $0.04 per $1,000 for maintenance, repairs, capital costs, and administration.
measures.cost_line: $4 annually per $100,000, or $20 on $500,000; the county estimates a $6.40 increase on $400,000.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says restoration maintains cemetery grounds and facilities.
measures.con_summary: No against committee formed; the tradeoff is cemetery services versus the levy increase.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds cemetery maintenance, repairs, capital costs, and administration.

measures.sources_note: Official local voters' pamphlet (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the fire levy to $1.25 per $1,000 for fire, EMS, apparatus, equipment, staffing, and safety.
measures.cost_line: $125 annually per $100,000, or $625 on $500,000; the current rate is about $0.75.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says restored funding supports staffing, equipment, training, and response.
measures.con_summary: No against committee formed; the tradeoff is emergency-service funding versus higher property tax.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES restores substantial funding for staffing, apparatus, equipment, training, response, and safety.

measures.sources_note: Official local voters' pamphlet (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the fire levy to as much as $1.50 per $1,000 and permits 104% annual growth for nine following years.
measures.cost_line: Initially up to $150 annually per $100,000, or $750 on $500,000; later collections may grow 4% annually.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee cites staffing, training, equipment, planning, and advanced-life-support development.
measures.con_summary: No against committee formed; the tradeoff is service stability versus a restored and growing levy.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds an explicit public-service or capital investment.

measures.sources_note: Official local voters' pamphlet (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the EMS levy to as much as $0.50 per $1,000 and permits 104% annual growth for nine following years.
measures.cost_line: Initially up to $50 annually per $100,000, or $250 on $500,000; later collections may grow 4% annually.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee cites supplies, training, vehicles, response, and advanced life support.
measures.con_summary: No against committee formed; the tradeoff is EMS funding versus a restored and growing levy.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds an explicit public-service or capital investment.

measures.sources_note: Official local voters' pamphlet (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Provides six years of dedicated funding for parks, recreation, community centers, fairgrounds, and 4-H.
measures.pro_summary: The county says the levy replaces unavailable funding for community programs and facilities.
measures.con_summary: No official opposition statement was available; the tradeoff is services versus a new six-year tax.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds an explicit public-service or capital investment.

measures.sources_note: Official local voters' pamphlet (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Annexes the City of Black Diamond into the Puget Sound Regional Fire Authority, which would take over fire protection and emergency medical services starting January 1, 2028, replacing the city's expiring contract with Mountain View Fire & Rescue.
measures.cost_line: Adds PSRFA's $1.00/$1,000 property tax levy plus a non-voted fire benefit charge; reported combined example of roughly $1,084/year for a 2,790 sq ft home assessed at $754,000.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Jonathan Englund) says joining PSRFA strengthens fire and EMS service as the community grows, with access to a larger regional system's 24/7 staffing, specialized equipment, and a more stable funding structure than a smaller standalone system.
measures.con_summary: The statement in opposition (Michael Carlin and Brian McGee, a former fire commissioner and career firefighters) says the measure creates a new tax while the city keeps money residents already pay for fire service, estimating about $1,083/year to start, without making response times faster.

taxes.basis: YES adds a permanent PSRFA levy plus fire benefit charge, a significantly higher fire tax than the current sub-$0.50/$1,000 contract cost, to fund regional service.

local-control.basis: YES trades a city-negotiated service contract for membership in a multi-city regional fire authority whose board sets future rates.

measures.sources_note: Pamphlet explanatory statement and ballot title (S1), pro/con committee statements (S2), and Courier-Herald coverage of costs and the annexation process (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Creates the Covington Park and Recreation District, a new metropolitan park district with the same boundaries as the city, authorized to levy up to $0.75 per $1,000 of assessed value (starting at $0.37) for parks, trails, and recreation facilities, with the city council serving as its board.
measures.cost_line: About $277.50/year (roughly $23/month) on a home assessed at $750,000 at the initial $0.37/$1,000 rate.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Casey Hogan, Kolawole Ogundari, and Laura Morrissey) says parks are falling behind while competing with police and roads for general-fund money, and that 100% of district revenue would be legally dedicated to Covington parks, including replacing the aging aquatic center.
measures.con_summary: The statement in opposition (Gary Cavanaugh) says residents already approved a utility tax tied to parks and are being asked to pay again, that it is not a good time for another new tax, and that the district could charge up to $0.75/$1,000 even though it starts at $0.37.

taxes.basis: YES creates a new junior taxing district with permanent regular property tax authority; the con case is centrally about resisting a new, ratcheting tax.

spending.basis: YES funds expanded park maintenance, improvements, and a planned aquatic/community center — public investment as the lever for amenity outcomes.

measures.sources_note: Pamphlet explanatory statement (S1), pro/con committee statements and rebuttals (S2), and the city's official CPRD page with cost estimates and project lists (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Replaces Seattle's expiring 2019 library levy with a seven-year property tax levy collecting $479.8 million from 2027 through 2033 to fund Seattle Public Library operating hours, collections, technology, maintenance, and administration.
measures.cost_line: Adds about $0.23 per $1,000 of assessed value — roughly $193/year for the median homeowner; the pro statement describes it as an additional $9/month for the average homeowner.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Katie B. Wilson, Joy Hollingsworth, and Ken Jennings) says the levy provides more than one third of total library funding, protecting programs, collections, and staffing, and funding seismic, accessibility, and energy upgrades including at the Central Library.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only one side.

taxes.basis: YES approves a levy lid lift funding library services — and an enlarged one, expanded about 17% above the mayor's proposal, not a flat renewal.

spending.basis: YES expands programs, seismic retrofits, security, and technology beyond the 2019 levy's baseline — public investment as the lever, with oversight provisions attached.

measures.sources_note: Pamphlet explanatory statement (S1), pro committee statement (S2), Ordinance 127419 full text (S3), the library's levy proposal page (S4), and council/news coverage of the package's expansion (S5, S6).

measures.what_it_does: Annexes King County Fire Protection District No. 43 (Maple Valley/Hobart area) into the Puget Sound Regional Fire Authority, which already serves the district by contract; the district would stop levying its own taxes and residents would instead pay the RFA's taxes and benefit charges, gaining board representation and a vote on future RFA measures.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated; the pamphlet does not itemize post-annexation rates for the district.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Andrew Montero, Warren Iverson) says annexation changes nothing about who responds to 911 calls but guarantees two seats on the RFA board and the right to vote on future RFA tax and benefit-charge measures the community currently has no say in.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only one side.

local-control.basis: YES folds an independent local district into a large regional authority for long-term stability, though it formalizes an existing service relationship and adds voting representation rather than changing who delivers service.

measures.sources_note: Pamphlet ballot title and explanatory statement (S1) and the pro committee statement (S2); Puget Sound Fire's site (S3) and Courier-Herald coverage of the parallel Black Diamond vote (S4) supply structural context only.

measures.what_it_does: Restores King County Fire Protection District No. 47's (Ravensdale area) regular property tax levy from about $0.54 to $1.00 per $1,000 of assessed value, with CPI-indexed growth allowed through 2031 and the lifted level made permanent in the levy base, to maintain current fire and EMS service levels.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated in the pamphlet; the increase is $0.46 per $1,000 of assessed value (for example, $46/year per $100,000 of assessed value).
measures.pro_summary: No statement in favor was filed; the district's official explanatory statement says the board determined the restoration is necessary to maintain current fire protection and EMS service levels and support firefighter safety.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only the district's own explanation.

taxes.basis: YES is a multi-year levy lid lift raising the property tax rate by $0.46/$1,000 with a permanently reset levy base to fund fire and EMS services.

measures.sources_note: Pamphlet ballot title and explanatory statement (S1); the pamphlet page noting no pro or con committee filed (S2); the district's website adds no further detail (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes Skykomish School District No. 404 to levy excess property taxes for four years (2027-2030, roughly $165,000 to $192,000 per year), replacing an expiring educational programs and operations levy that funds operations the state does not, such as sports, transportation, technology, and maintenance.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated in the pamphlet; the estimated rate is $0.45 per $1,000 of assessed value, lower than the expiring levy's estimated $0.61.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Sarah Bender, for Skykomish Citizens for Quality Schools) says voting yes keeps classrooms and facilities safe, warm, and dry, funds security components and technology, and describes maintaining the district's historic facilities as fiscally responsible.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only one side.

taxes.basis: YES continues taxing to fund school operations, but as a replacement levy at a lower estimated rate than the expiring one it is continuation rather than a new or larger tax.

measures.sources_note: Pamphlet explanatory statement (S1), pro committee statement (S2), and King County Elections records of the predecessor 2022 levy (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Continues Snoqualmie Pass Fire and Rescue's existing benefit charge — a fee scaled to structure size and risk rather than assessed value — for six more years, replacing the expiring charge voters approved in 2020; while in effect, the district gives up $0.50/$1,000 of property tax authority.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated; the charge is set annually per property based on building size and risk, capped at 60% of the district's operating budget.
measures.pro_summary: No statement in favor was filed; the district's official explanatory statement presents the board's view that a benefit charge paired with lower property taxes is the fairest, most stable, and most cost-effective way to finance district operations.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only the district's own explanation.
measures.sources_note: Pamphlet ballot title and explanatory statement (S1); the pamphlet page noting no pro or con committee filed (S2); Snoqualmie Valley Record coverage of the 2020 renewal for history only (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Raises Bremerton's regular property-tax rate by $0.48 to an authorized $1.95 per $1,000 for 2027, permanently resetting the later levy base to fund public safety and other city services.
measures.cost_line: Gross increase of $48 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $240 on $500,000; the pro committee says expiring levies reduce the net increase to $0.39 per $1,000 in 2027 and $0.26 thereafter.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the levy would hire nine firefighters, replace fire vehicles, modernize Station 1, and establish an equipment-replacement fund.
measures.con_summary: The con committee calls the increase permanent and excessive and favors technology, lighter response vehicles, and budget reprioritization.

taxes.basis: YES permanently raises and resets the city's regular property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds added staffing, vehicle replacement, station modernization, and a continuing equipment fund.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title, explanation, and pro/con committee arguments (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes up to $7.64 million in six-year general-obligation bonds for fleet-facility work, fire and EMS vehicles, and safety equipment, repaid through excess property taxes.
measures.cost_line: Estimated $0.18 per $1,000, or $7.92 monthly for the average North Kitsap home assessed at $528,283.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the fleet facility, engine, medic units, and marine unit require renovation or replacement under the long-term plan.
measures.con_summary: No against committee formed, so the pamphlet contains no organized opposition argument.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a new short-term excess property-tax levy to repay capital bonds.

spending.basis: YES makes a defined capital investment in facilities, vehicles, and safety equipment.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title, explanation, and pro statement (S1); no con committee formed.

measures.what_it_does: Continues for six years a dedicated EMS levy at an initial rate up to $0.50 per $1,000 beginning in 2027, with later growth generally limited to one percent.
measures.cost_line: Up to $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 annually on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the longstanding levy supports paramedics, EMTs, ambulance transport, equipment, training, and round-the-clock readiness.
measures.con_summary: No against committee formed, so the pamphlet contains no organized opposition argument.

taxes.basis: YES renews an existing six-year dedicated property-tax levy rather than creating a new tax.

spending.basis: YES maintains dedicated EMS staffing, transport, equipment, training, and readiness.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title, explanation, and pro statement (S1); no con committee formed.

measures.what_it_does: Lifts the city fire-service levy lid for two years, adding $0.98 and setting the rate at $1.87 per $1,000.
measures.cost_line: At the total rate, $187 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $935 on $500,000; the increase is $98 per $100,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies city fire service as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is city fire revenue versus a higher property-tax rate.

taxes.basis: YES adds $0.98 per $1,000 through a two-year levy lid lift.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a city library levy at up to $1.50 per $1,000 of assessed value.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum rate, $150 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $750 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies library service as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is library revenue versus the authorized property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a city library property-tax levy of up to $1.50 per $1,000.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Lifts the district's regular fire levy lid to as much as $1.50 per $1,000.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum rate, $150 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $750 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies fire and emergency operations as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is fire-service revenue versus a higher property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES lifts the regular fire-levy lid to a stated maximum rate.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Continues the fire district benefit charge for six years, allowing it to fund up to 60% of the operating budget.
measures.cost_line: The charge may fund up to 60% of the operating budget; no uniform parcel rate is stated because charges vary by property characteristics.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies fire and emergency operations as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is continued operating revenue versus continued property charges.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing fire-district benefit charge for six years.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Sets the district fire and EMS levy at $1.15 per $1,000 and authorizes CPI adjustments for nine years.
measures.cost_line: $115 annually per $100,000, or $575 on $500,000, before later CPI adjustments.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named local-service funding; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES sets a property-tax rate and authorizes nine years of CPI adjustments.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Continues a six-year EMS levy at $0.45 per $1,000 of assessed value.
measures.cost_line: $45 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $225 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the revenue for EMS operations; no pro statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was available; the tradeoff is EMS funding versus continued property taxes.

taxes.basis: YES continues or renews an existing property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Restores the district EMS levy to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value.
measures.cost_line: $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the revenue for EMS operations; no pro statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was available; the tradeoff is EMS funding versus higher property taxes.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a substantial property-tax levy or debt obligation.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Restores the fire and EMS levy to $0.88 per $1,000 and permits nine years of CPI adjustments.
measures.cost_line: Initially $88 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $440 on $500,000; later collections may adjust with CPI.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the revenue for fire and EMS operations; no pro statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was available; the tradeoff is fire and EMS funding versus higher, CPI-adjusted property taxes.

taxes.basis: YES restores or creates a substantial property-tax levy or debt obligation.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Restores the regular fire and EMS levy to as much as $1.48 per $1,000 and permits a 104% limit factor for three years.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum initial rate, $148 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $740 on $500,000; later collections may grow under the limit factor.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies fire protection and EMS operations as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is fire and EMS revenue versus a restored and growing property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES restores the levy to a stated maximum and permits three years of 104% growth.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Renews and increases the district EMS levy to as much as $0.50 per $1,000 for 2027 through 2032.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum rate, $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies EMS operations as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is EMS revenue versus an increased six-year property-tax levy.

taxes.basis: YES both renews and increases the EMS property-tax levy for six years.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Continues the authority's EMS levy at up to $0.50 per $1,000 for six years.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum rate, $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies EMS operations as the use; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is continued EMS revenue versus continued property tax.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing EMS property-tax levy for six years.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Lifts the county regular property-tax levy lid for 2027 collections.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named public-service revenue; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES lifts the regular property-tax levy lid to the stated rate.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Continues the fire district EMS property-tax levy for six years.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named public-service revenue; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing EMS property-tax levy for six years.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Continues the City of Pateros EMS property-tax levy for six years.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named public-service revenue; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing municipal EMS property-tax levy for six years.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Renews a one-year special property-tax levy for hospital-district operations.
measures.cost_line: Raises $1,460,000 at about $0.33 per $1,000, or about $165 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies the named public-service revenue; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the tradeoff is the named service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES renews a one-year special hospital-district property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1); no committee statements were available.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes up to $10 million in general-obligation bonds, maturing within 25 years, to replace the regional fire authority headquarters station.
measures.cost_line: Up to $10 million in bonds repaid through annual excess property-tax levies over no more than 25 years; the official summary does not state a uniform tax rate.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies replacement of the headquarters fire station as the use; no committee argument was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee argument was available; the direct tradeoff is a replacement public-safety facility versus long-term debt and excess property-tax levies.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes annual excess property-tax levies to repay up to $10 million in bonds.

spending.basis: YES authorizes a major long-term public capital investment in a replacement headquarters station.

measures.sources_note: Official Pacific County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes 30-year general-obligation bonds to expand and renovate Newport Community Hospital.
measures.cost_line: Up to $51 million in bonds repaid through excess property-tax levies over no more than 30 years.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies hospital expansion and renovation as the use; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the direct tradeoff is the hospital project versus long-term bond and property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes excess property-tax levies to repay up to $51 million in bonds.

spending.basis: YES authorizes a major long-term public capital investment in hospital expansion and renovation.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Reauthorizes Carbonado to levy up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value for six years to continue basic and advanced emergency medical services.
measures.cost_line: Up to $50 annually per $100,000 of assessed value, or $250 on a $500,000 property, before applicable exemptions.
measures.pro_summary: The official measure materials present the levy as continuing local EMT and paramedic services through a dedicated revenue source.
measures.con_summary: No statement against was identified in the official pamphlet material reviewed; the principal trade-off is continuing the property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES continues a dedicated local property tax for six years; it is a renewal rather than a new or larger stated rate.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet material (S1) and the sample ballot (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Renews Fircrest's authority to levy up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value from 2027 through 2032 for emergency medical services delivered under its Tacoma service contract.
measures.cost_line: Up to $50 annually per $100,000 of assessed value, or $250 on a $500,000 property.
measures.pro_summary: The city resolution says uninterrupted levy authority is needed to continue emergency medical care and services under the Tacoma service contract.
measures.con_summary: No opposition statement was identified in the official material reviewed; the measurable trade-off is continuation of the property levy.

taxes.basis: YES renews a six-year property tax dedicated to EMS rather than creating permanent authority.

measures.sources_note: Fircrest Resolution No. 2020 (S1) and official pamphlet material (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Reinstates for ten years a 1.5% utility tax and a $0.20-per-$1,000 property-tax increase to fund Connect Tacoma school routes, paving, sidewalks, maintenance, lighting, traffic calming, ADA, multimodal, greenway, and neighborhood projects.
measures.cost_line: The official estimate is $101.52 annually in property tax plus $23.64 in utility tax for the average homeowner, about $125.16 combined; some residents may qualify for property-tax exemptions.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the prior program leveraged partner funding and improved school crossings and streets, and that renewal funds safer access, potholes, and neighborhood projects.
measures.con_summary: The con committee says too little is reserved for neighborhood paving, the safety category is insufficiently defined, Tacoma already has a high tax burden and budget deficit, and a narrower road-repair measure would be preferable.

taxes.basis: YES reinstates both utility and property taxes for ten years, even though they replace an expired tax structure.

spending.basis: YES dedicates substantial revenue to a broad transportation capital, safety, maintenance, accessibility, and multimodal program.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet explanation and pro/con statements (S1) and Tacoma Resolution No. 41894 as incorporated in the ballot title (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes up to $25 million in general-obligation bonds maturing within 20 years to acquire, construct, and equip a replacement fire station, repaid through annual excess property taxes.
measures.cost_line: The official material states the maximum principal and repayment mechanism but gives no household estimate or projected tax rate.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the existing station is undersized and outdated, lacks modern decontamination and exhaust separation, and cannot accommodate a diverse workforce; replacement would improve health and operations.
measures.con_summary: No statement against was submitted; the central unargued trade-off is up to $25 million in debt and the excess property taxes required for repayment.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes additional property taxes over as long as 20 years to repay new bond debt.

spending.basis: YES makes a major public-safety capital investment in a replacement station; the measure is facility-specific rather than a broad program expansion.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title and committee statement (S1) and District Resolution No. 444 as summarized in the ballot title (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Replaces an expiring levy for educational programs and operations not fully funded by the state for 2027-2030.
measures.cost_line: Estimated $0.36 per $1,000 annually, or $180 on $500,000; collections rise from $1,115,000 to $1,235,000.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee cites nursing, library, advanced courses, garden, projects, staffing, and classroom resources.
measures.con_summary: The opposition argues against continuing local property-tax burdens; the tradeoff is programs versus a four-year levy.

taxes.basis: YES continues or replaces an existing property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds an explicit public-service or capital investment.

measures.sources_note: Official local voters' pamphlet (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Replaces an expiring school operations levy with a four-year levy collecting up to $950,000 annually from 2027 through 2030 for staffing, student programs, transportation, supplies, training, and other operations not fully state-funded.
measures.cost_line: Estimated rates decline from $1.24 per $1,000 of assessed value in 2027 to $1.02 in 2030; supporters describe it as a no-increase replacement.
measures.pro_summary: Supporters say the replacement preserves athletics, paraeducators, transportation, maintenance, and other school and community-center services without increasing the fixed annual collection.
measures.con_summary: Opponents say compensation costs and academic results warrant rejecting the levy and forcing the district to prioritize spending and rebuild parent trust.

taxes.basis: YES continues a local property-tax levy for school operations, but replaces an expiring levy without increasing the fixed annual collection.

spending.basis: YES sustains staffing, student programs, transportation, and other operations, choosing continued public investment over the opposition's requested retrenchment.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot explanation and pro/con statements (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the district's regular property-tax levy to no more than $1.25 per $1,000 in 2027, permits 3% annual growth for five years, and makes the final-year amount the later levy base for fire and EMS staffing and equipment.
measures.cost_line: About $180 annually on $500,000 assessed value, based on an initial increase of approximately $0.36 per $1,000.
measures.pro_summary: Supporters cite rising emergency calls and reduced volunteer availability and say the levy would add two full-time firefighters and help replace response vehicles.
measures.con_summary: No opposition statement was filed.

taxes.basis: YES raises the property-tax rate, allows above-default growth for five years, and permanently resets the levy base.

spending.basis: YES expands staffing and equipment investment to respond to higher call volume.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot explanation and supporting statement (S1); no opposition statement was filed.

measures.what_it_does: Raises District 304's regular property-tax levy from about $0.09 to $0.50 per $1,000 beginning in 2027 and resets the later levy base to fund local prevention, nutrition, family, youth, mental-health, senior, and health-access programs.
measures.cost_line: About $17 per month on a $500,000 home, based on an increase of approximately $0.41 per $1,000.
measures.pro_summary: Supporters say stable local revenue would preserve and expand prevention and wellness programs while allowing residents to set local health priorities.
measures.con_summary: No opposition statement was filed.

taxes.basis: YES substantially raises the district's regular property-tax rate and permanently resets its levy base.

spending.basis: YES provides stable expanded public funding across a broad range of health, prevention, and family programs.

local-control.basis: YES raises revenue retained by the local hospital district so local residents can set health-service priorities rather than relying on restricted outside grants.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet explanation and supporting statement (S1) plus the district's levy information (S2); no opposition statement was filed.

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a one-year excess levy replacing an expiring levy for water-system maintenance and capital improvements.
measures.cost_line: Raises $25,000 for 2027 at approximately $0.57 per $1,000, or $285 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies water maintenance and capital improvements as the use; no committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No committee statement was available; the direct tradeoff is water-system funding versus the one-year excess levy.

taxes.basis: YES replaces an expiring one-year excess property-tax levy.

spending.basis: YES funds water-system maintenance and capital improvements.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores Everett's EMS property-tax levy from about $0.36 to $0.50 per $1,000 for 2027 and 2028 and resets the future levy base.
measures.cost_line: The pro committee estimates under $7 monthly, about $80 annually, for an average $571,000 Everett property.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the restoration maintains paramedic and EMT service for EMS calls, which constitute nearly 82% of fire responses.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES raises the EMS property-tax rate and resets its future levy base.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Creates a 0.1% sales and use tax for current and enhanced police, dispatch, court, prosecution, defense, jail, and support services.
measures.cost_line: One dollar per $1,000 of taxable retail purchases; the city estimates about $300,000 in 2027 revenue.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says population growth and rising contracted-service costs require dedicated public-safety revenue and that visitors will share the cost.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES creates a new local sales and use tax.

spending.basis: YES provides dedicated revenue for current and enhanced public-safety and justice services.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Replaces an expiring four-year school levy with annual collections of $950,000 for staffing, class size, counselors, arts, athletics, music, transportation, supplies, and operations.
measures.cost_line: Estimated rates decline from $1.24 per $1,000 in 2027 to $1.02 in 2030.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee describes it as a no-increase replacement needed to preserve programs and staffing.
measures.con_summary: The con committee says spending and academic outcomes are unsustainable and urges reprioritization before renewal.

taxes.basis: YES replaces an expiring property-tax levy at the described no-increase collection level.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro/con statements (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a four-year excess property-tax levy collecting $450,000 annually for fire, EMS, and rescue operations.
measures.cost_line: Estimated rates range from $0.6214 per $1,000 in 2027 to $0.6031 in 2030.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says stable funding preserves staffing, equipment, response times, and existing service.
measures.con_summary: The con committee opposes additional taxes and asks the district to pursue efficiencies or alternatives.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a four-year excess property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro/con statements (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the EMS levy from about $0.32 to $0.50 per $1,000 in 2027, allows up to 6% annual growth through 2032, and resets the later levy base.
measures.cost_line: The 2027 rate increase is about $0.18 per $1,000; no model-home cost is stated.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says EMS accounts for more than 80% of calls and current revenue does not cover operating costs.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES restores a higher property-tax rate, permits six years of levy growth, and resets the base.

spending.basis: YES expands EMS operating revenue to address the funding gap described by proponents.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes up to $382 million in 30-year bonds and excess property taxes to construct and equip a replacement EvergreenHealth Monroe hospital.
measures.cost_line: Estimated average rate of $0.437 per $1,000, about $40.75 monthly for a $745,000 home.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee cites aging facilities, overcrowding, capacity, technology, local access, audits, and project oversight.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes excess property taxes to repay up to $382 million in long-term bonds.

spending.basis: YES makes a large long-term public capital investment in a replacement hospital.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Raises the library levy from about $0.304 to $0.47 per $1,000 in 2027, resets the future base, and funds services, staffing, materials, technology, mobile service, and facilities.
measures.cost_line: Estimated increase of $124.25 annually, or $10.35 monthly, for a $748,100 home.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the levy sustains and expands learning, access, collections, staffing, mobile service, and safe facilities.
measures.con_summary: The con committee calls it a 55% tax increase, says spending has outpaced inflation, and argues priorities should be narrowed.

taxes.basis: YES substantially raises the library property-tax rate and resets its future levy base.

spending.basis: YES expands the revenue base for library staffing, services, collections, technology, and facilities rather than narrowing priorities.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro/con statements (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the permanent EMS levy to $0.50 per $1,000 for 2027 and allows CPI-based levy growth for the next five years.
measures.cost_line: No model-home cost is stated in the pamphlet.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says restoration maintains personnel, vehicles, equipment, training, response times, and stable service.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES restores a higher permanent levy rate and permits five years of inflation-linked growth.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Renews for 14 years Cheney's 16.75% tax on electrical-energy and natural-gas businesses, including six percentage points dedicated to residential street and sidewalk repair.
measures.cost_line: No model household cost is stated; the measure continues a six-percentage-point utility-business tax increment through the 14-year term.
measures.pro_summary: No pro committee filed; the city explanation says dedicated revenue is needed to preserve residential streets and sidewalks and support travel safety.
measures.con_summary: No con committee filed; the trade-off is continuation of the utility tax, which may be passed through to customers, versus dedicated maintenance funding, with no pass-through estimate provided.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing utility-business tax increment for 14 years rather than allowing the rate to fall.

spending.basis: YES preserves dedicated public funding for residential street and sidewalk repair.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title and explanation (S1) and the linked city resolution (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes Rosalia Park and Recreation District No. 5 to collect $85,000 in each of 2027 and 2028 for pool operations, maintenance, improvements, supplies, salaries, and utilities.
measures.cost_line: Estimated at $0.39 per $1,000 of assessed value, about $117 annually on a $300,000 property; the stated maximum is $0.60 per $1,000.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says stable funding supports maintenance, trained lifeguards, affordable access, safe recreation, and avoidance of larger deferred repairs.
measures.con_summary: No con committee filed; the trade-off is a temporary property-tax levy versus reduced hours, higher user fees, or facility deterioration without operating revenue.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a two-year property-tax levy for pool operations.

spending.basis: YES uses dedicated public funding to maintain operations, staffing, access, and improvements at a community pool.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title, explanation, and pro statement (S1), plus the linked district resolution (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the library district's regular property-tax levy from approximately $0.33 to $0.45 per $1,000 for 2027, establishing a new basis for future levy limits and funding operations, materials, facilities, accessibility, and security.
measures.cost_line: The pro statement estimates about $3.50 per month for a $350,000 home.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says inflation threatens staffing, hours, programs, maintenance, and accessibility improvements across ten branches and mobile services.
measures.con_summary: No con committee filed; the trade-off is the higher property-tax bill versus the service reductions the district says would occur if the levy fails.

taxes.basis: YES raises the district's property-tax rate and resets the base for future levy limits.

spending.basis: YES expands the revenue base used for library staffing, hours, materials, facilities, accessibility, and security.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot title, explanation, and pro statement (S1), plus the linked library resolution (S2).

measures.what_it_does: Reauthorizes an existing Spokane Transit Authority sales and use tax of up to 0.2% from 2029 through no later than 2048 for fixed-route, paratransit, rideshare, service expansion, programs, and facilities.
measures.cost_line: Up to 20 cents per $100 of taxable purchases, continuing the maximum rate approved in 2016 rather than increasing it.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the tax supported service expansion, stations, and City Line and would fund Division Street bus rapid transit, mobility pilots, longer hours, and wider coverage.
measures.con_summary: The con committee says STA's reserves grew above $270 million and argues existing revenues and reserves should be used before extending the tax for up to 20 years.

taxes.basis: YES continues an existing 0.2% sales tax for up to 20 additional years without raising its maximum rate.

spending.basis: YES secures long-range funding for continued and expanded public transportation rather than first drawing down reserves.

measures.sources_note: Official ballot title and explanation (S1), pro/con statements and rebuttals (S2), and the linked STA resolution (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Creates a West Plains Aquifer Protection Area for 20 years and authorizes water-withdrawal and on-site-sewage fees to raise about $400,000 annually for monitoring, planning, education, water-quality facilities, and preventive work.
measures.cost_line: Household fees are capped at $1.25 per month for water withdrawal and $1.25 per month for on-site sewage disposal; non-household water fees vary by meter size.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says growth, runoff, disposal, and spills threaten the aquifers and that dedicated science, planning, and prevention funding can reduce future harm.
measures.con_summary: The con committee says contaminated residents should not pay, the measure will not clean existing contamination, and responsible airport and Air Force entities should fund cleanup.

taxes.basis: YES imposes new user fees for a 20-year aquifer-protection program, with household caps stated in the ballot materials.

spending.basis: YES creates a dedicated preventive planning, monitoring, education, and water-quality program rather than limiting action to polluter-funded remediation.

measures.sources_note: Official ballot title and explanation (S1), pro/con statements and rebuttal (S2), and the linked county resolution (S3).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a property-tax levy for fire-protection services in Stevens County Fire Protection District No. 6.
measures.cost_line: The official title establishes a fire-service property tax; no reliable rate or model-home cost estimate is stated.
measures.pro_summary: The stated purpose is continued fire-protection service funding.
measures.con_summary: No official con statement was available; the trade-off is fire-service funding versus property-tax cost.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a property-tax levy, but the dossier does not establish whether it raises a current rate or its magnitude.

measures.sources_note: Official election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes $21.01 million in general-obligation bonds maturing within 25 years to renovate four fire stations and acquire an ambulance, tender/pumper, and other apparatus.
measures.cost_line: The pro committee estimates about $0.20 per $1,000, or $8.33 monthly for a $500,000 home.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says the projects expand staffing and response capacity and add exhaust-removal and decontamination safety systems.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes property taxes to repay $21.01 million in long-term bonds.

spending.basis: YES makes a substantial public capital investment in stations, ambulances, and firefighting apparatus.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a four-year maintenance and operations levy at about $0.38 per $1,000 to maintain fire and EMS service in Rochester-Grand Mound.
measures.cost_line: About $15.83 monthly for a $500,000 home; annual collections rise from $826,166 in 2027 to $956,500 in 2030.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says planned apparatus replacement and facility maintenance preserve readiness and reduce emergency repair costs.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a four-year property-tax levy for maintenance and operations.

spending.basis: YES supplies planned funding for apparatus replacement, facility maintenance, and emergency readiness.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a four-year maintenance and operations levy at about $0.38 per $1,000 to maintain fire and EMS service in Littlerock-Maytown.
measures.cost_line: About $15.83 monthly for a $500,000 home; annual collections rise from $807,020 in 2027 to $934,226 in 2030.
measures.pro_summary: The pro committee says predictable apparatus replacement and building maintenance preserve 24/7 readiness and avoid larger failures.
measures.con_summary: No con statement was submitted after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a four-year property-tax levy for maintenance and operations.

spending.basis: YES supplies predictable funding for apparatus replacement, building maintenance, and readiness.

measures.sources_note: Official pamphlet ballot material and pro statement (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Sets the regular fire levy at $1.10 per $1,000 and authorizes a 104% limit factor for six years.
measures.cost_line: At the stated rate, $110 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $550 on $500,000; this is arithmetic from the official rate, not a model-home estimate.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies fire and emergency-service funding; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the trade-off is fire-service revenue versus higher property tax.

taxes.basis: YES sets a property-tax rate and permits six years of levy growth.

measures.sources_note: Official Whatcom County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the cross-county hospital district's regular levy to $0.50 per $1,000 for 2027 and resets the later levy base.
measures.cost_line: At the stated rate, $50 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $250 on $500,000; this is arithmetic from the official rate, not a model-home estimate.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies hospital-district funding; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the trade-off is public-health funding versus higher property tax.

taxes.basis: YES restores a higher levy rate and resets the future property-tax base.

measures.sources_note: Official Whatcom County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores the regular fire levy to $1.48 per $1,000 and authorizes a 106% limit factor for nine years.
measures.cost_line: At the stated maximum, $148 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $740 on $500,000; this is arithmetic from the official rate, not a model-home estimate.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies local fire-protection funding; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the trade-off is higher and growing property-tax revenue versus taxpayer restraint.

taxes.basis: YES restores the levy rate and permits nine years of up to 6% levy growth.

measures.sources_note: Official Whatcom County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Sets the regular fire levy at $1.20 per $1,000 and authorizes a 103% limit factor for five years.
measures.cost_line: At the stated rate, $120 annually per $100,000 assessed value, or $600 on $500,000; this is arithmetic from the official rate, not a model-home estimate.
measures.pro_summary: Official material identifies local fire-protection funding; no pro committee statement was available.
measures.con_summary: No con committee statement was available; the trade-off is fire-service revenue versus higher property tax.

taxes.basis: YES sets a property-tax rate and permits five years of levy growth.

measures.sources_note: Official Whatcom County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Annexes the City of Tekoa Fire Department into Whitman County Fire Protection District No. 1 if both jurisdictions approve.
measures.cost_line: City residents become subject to the district tax levy and eligible to vote in district elections; no model-home estimate is stated.
measures.pro_summary: The explanation says annexation supports direct district service, long-term planning, and service delivery.
measures.con_summary: No pro or con volunteers submitted statements after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES subjects city residents to the district property-tax levy, with no rate or model-home estimate documented.

local-control.basis: YES transfers city fire service into a county fire-protection district and gives residents district voting rights.

measures.sources_note: Official Whitman County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes two years of taxes for operating, maintaining, and improving the Rosalia Pool.
measures.cost_line: Raises $85,000 in each of 2027 and 2028 at an estimated $0.39 per $1,000, capped at $0.60; about $195 annually on $500,000 at the estimate.
measures.pro_summary: The pro statement says stable funding supports maintenance, lifeguards, access, public health, and avoiding larger deferred repairs.
measures.con_summary: No against statement was submitted.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a two-year property-tax levy for pool operations and improvements.

spending.basis: YES dedicates public funding to pool operations, staffing, maintenance, access, and improvements.

measures.sources_note: Official Whitman County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Raises $28,000 for street maintenance, repairs and improvements and the town current-expense fund.
measures.cost_line: Estimated at $4.18 or less per $1,000 for 2027 collection, or about $2,090 on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: The ballot identifies street work and the current-expense fund as the uses of revenue.
measures.con_summary: No official committee arguments were available.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a high-rate excess property-tax levy for 2027.

spending.basis: YES provides dedicated revenue for street work and current expenses.

measures.sources_note: Official Whitman County sample ballot (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Annexes the City of Tekoa into Fire Protection District No. 1 if both jurisdictions approve.
measures.cost_line: City residents become subject to the district tax levy and eligible to vote in district elections; no model-home estimate is stated.
measures.pro_summary: The explanation says annexation supports direct district service, long-term planning, and service delivery.
measures.con_summary: No pro or con volunteers submitted statements after recruitment.

taxes.basis: YES subjects annexed city residents to the district property-tax levy, with no rate documented.

local-control.basis: YES consolidates city fire service into a county fire-protection district.

measures.sources_note: Official Whitman County election material (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Authorizes a four-year levy for fire-protection maintenance and operations.
measures.cost_line: Raises $10,000 annually from 2027 through 2030 at approximately $0.21 per $1,000, or about $105 annually on $500,000.
measures.pro_summary: The ballot identifies fire-protection maintenance and operations as the use.
measures.con_summary: No official committee arguments were available.

taxes.basis: YES authorizes a four-year property-tax levy.

measures.sources_note: Official Whitman County sample ballot (S1).

measures.what_it_does: Restores Fire Protection District No. 6's regular property-tax levy in 2027 to no more than $1.00 per $1,000, permits a 103% annual levy limit factor for nine years, and makes the final maximum the base for later levy limits.
measures.cost_line: At the maximum stated rate, $100 annually per $100,000 of assessed value, or $500 on a $500,000 property; later bills depend on levy and assessed value.
measures.pro_summary: No pro-committee statement was available; the official ballot says the measure restores the district's regular levy for local fire-protection funding.
measures.con_summary: No con-committee statement was available; the direct trade-off is higher and potentially growing property-tax collections versus district fire-protection revenue.

taxes.basis: YES restores the levy up to $1.00 per $1,000, permits up to 3% annual levy growth for nine years, and permanently resets the later levy base.

measures.sources_note: Official sample-ballot title (S1); no pro or con committee statements were available in the reviewed election sources.
